body:
I think the `Math.round` is rounding always up for it, maybe we should then use `Math.ceil`?

Why don't we omit it at all? Slice would use the Integer (floor) number of it, wouldn't this be fine too?
author: BHesseldieck

body: ref previous comment about `Math.round`
author: BHesseldieck

body:
Thanks for bringing this up - in fact, it does get the integer part only. I changed to `Math.ceil` to make it clear that we're always rounding up not to generate confusion.

The reason not to omit is that more characters is always better than less. Esp for shorter strings, it makes a lot of difference, so I think using ceil works best.
author: krynble

body:
If you want to have more than you should use `Math.floor` or even omit the rounding, since slice will round down and take the integer value of it example:

```js
var password = '1234567891011'
password.slice(Math.ceil(password.length / 2)) --> '891011'
password.slice(Math.floor(password.length / 2)) --> '7891011'
password.slice(password.length / 2) --> '7891011'
```
author: BHesseldieck

body:
@Joffcom sorry for the delay ! 

It's not easy to share an example workflow because I obviously have specific node which read encoded protobuf and parse it it JSON, and I can't share it here. 

But my workflow is like that : 
```
{ "nodes": [ { "parameters": { "topic": "identity-events", "groupId": "n8n-client-id", "options": {} }, "name": "Kafka Trigger", "type": "n8n-nodes-base.kafkaTrigger", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 460, 300 ], "credentials": { "kafka": { "id": "1", "name": "Kafka account" } } }, { "parameters": { "message": "message", "targetField": "identityEvent" }, "name": "Identity Event Decoder", "type": "@swan-io/n8n-nodes-swan.identityEventDecoder", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 680, 300 ] }, { "parameters": {}, "name": "NoOp", "type": "n8n-nodes-base.noOp", "typeVersion": 1, "position": [ 900, 300 ] } ], "connections": { "Kafka Trigger": { "main": [ [ { "node": "Identity Event Decoder", "type": "main", "index": 0 } ] ] }, "Identity Event Decoder": { "main": [ [ { "node": "NoOp", "type": "main", "index": 0 } ] ] } } }

```

The node `Identity Event Decoder` will transform a protobuf buffer (I use https://github.com/stephenh/ts-proto for encoding/decoding) in a JS object. 

And with the default behaviour, I think the toString method can alter buffer sometimes, and then the decoding no longer works

body:
In addition to the dir case, there are potentially more levels of nesting: dir > component > symbol > toast. Rephrasing a display string, renaming a component or dir, refactoring a component, they all require updating the translation file.

I don't disagree that this coupling is not ideal, but if we flatten the object, the translations file becomes less organized, and we lose the ability to translate the same keys in different ways when in different contexts. Enforcing nesting to avoid key clashes was the motivation for reworking how nested node params are translated in the latest i18n refactorings.

Changed the function to accept string or number for workflow ID and started query from shared workflows table, making it simpler to execute.

In regards to making it ready for sharing, actually this is a huge change because all runtime checks will be different then, and will have to receive a list of users instead of a single user.

Due to this reason, we are following the premise that each workflow contains one and only one owner.
author: krynble

body:
It is not a sanitized version, actually we need a representation of the user residing in the `workflow` package.

Similar to what we have as `packages/cli/src/databases/entities/WorkflowEntity.ts` that is a database entity representing a workflow and `packages/workflow/src/Workflow.ts` which is a similar object (contains the same properties but more methods).

Perhaps it would be better to follow this pattern and rename:
- `User` entity in the `cli` package becomes `UserEntity`
- `N8nUserData` in the `workflow` package becomes `User`

body:
Cornelius, thank you for your review.

I find it reasonable that `getInstanceOwner` would itself take care of this unlikely case to ensure the call always succeeds. Looking at other commands, we do not follow a pattern of breaking commands down into atomic methods. Also, `getInstanceOwner` was merged like this in the reset command: https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/2698

Please let me know if you recommend the change anyway and I'll update it everywhere: here, in reset, and in import workflows.

body:
Changed length to 36.

Using `CHAR` makes no difference; it is only worth it if your entire row is fixed width, i.e. if you have a single field that is `varchar` then the performance gains from `char` are all lost.

body: [This rule](https://github.com/import-js/eslint-plugin-import/blob/main/docs/rules/no-cycle.md) treads the line between style and architecture/organization. I agree with keeping it to avoid circular dependencies, but fixing it throughout the project will require significant refactoring, so until then file-wide exceptions for this rule look reasonable, but let me know otherwise.

body: `addRoutes` applies the middleware and these four endpoint namespaces to the regular server, but the test server needs the middleware and only the specific namespace(s) relevant to the test suite that the test server is in. Middleware is always needed for UM tests, hence the need to stop loading all namespaces by default for the test server.
author: ivov

body: Specifically, the role expectation differs. But more generally, I view shell, owner and member as separate requests because the role dictates so much of may happen from now on, and that will be even more so the case as we keep adding more role-dependent logic. Let me know if you'd like them merged (branching for role expectation), but it gives me peace of mind that we have fully isolated member tests for all endpoints.

body:
@janober there's no change in data returned. Just a better error message when there is a `get` request on a folder (not a file) with `asBinaryProperty` enabled. Without this, the code would (does) error out anyway because there's no `responseData.content` on [L2237](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/2752/files#diff-2271b836ca282a11927f0f973b16db542596981180e82ba57d057fcbf68d6e71R2237).

I also checked and confirmed that the objects in the array don't have a `content` property... presumably because the `content` of a folder is this array of files/folders.
author: pemontto

body:
No change in functionality when `asBinaryProperty` is false, but when it's true and `File Path` is a folder.

Error before:
```
ERROR: The first argument must be of type string or an instance of Buffer, ArrayBuffer, or Array or an Array-like Object. Received undefined

TypeError [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE]: The first argument must be of type string or an instance of Buffer, ArrayBuffer, or Array or an Array-like Object. Received undefined
    at Function.from (buffer.js:333:9)
    at Object.execute (/usr/local/lib/node_modules/n8n/node_modules/n8n-nodes-base/dist/nodes/Github/Github.node.js:1889:106)
    at processTicksAndRejections (internal/process/task_queues.js:93:5)
    at async /usr/local/lib/node_modules/n8n/node_modules/n8n-core/dist/src/WorkflowExecute.js:447:47
```

Error after:
`ERROR: File Path is a folder, not a file.`

body:
Tested locally and it seems fine for when all services are running, but then I tested stopping the Redis container and the health check endpoint won't fail - it will stay stuck in a loop until redis is back or either the other mechanisms stop the instance.

Considering the instance stops automatically I don't think we need to change anything but it's an interesting aspect: it won't fail immediately if Redis is unreachable.

I left 2 other comments but these are just considerations, not things I think that need to be changed.

body:
Apologies for the glitch that removed your comment. You were asking to remove this line.

Afaik `POST /users/:id` (originally `POST /user`) is authless, so this exception

```ts
(req.method === 'POST' && new RegExp(`/${restEndpoint}/users/[\\w\\d-]*`).test(req.url))
``` 

is needed to prevent Passport from returning 401 for `POST /users/:id`. Removing this exception restores protection for the endpoint, causing all three tests to break as expected.

Did you mean something else?

body: Not exactly. The type guard specifies not only that `req.user` is not `undefined` but also that `req.user` is of type `User`, which allows for role access without the assertion. Without the type guard, in the second half we know that `req.user` is not `undefined` but not what type it is.

body:
Renamed as suggested, but please consider:
- File searchability. This is a monorepo where we already have four different `credentials.ts` files that match on file search - even searching for `credentials name` (file plus part of new dirname) actually brings up `CredentialsSelectModal.vue` first. On the other hand, using a dotted suffix like `.endpoints` brings up the file already on `credentials.e`, followed also by the relevant tests `credentials.endpoints.test.ts`, with all other similar but irrelevant `credentials` files getting filtered out. We could use `.api` instead of `.endpoints` to keep it concise, ideally mirrored in the tests file.
- Consistency. I think this bundle of endpoints does not constitute a namespace (e.g. thinking of wildcard `*` imports, TS types grouped in namespaces, etc.) because  in `Server.ts` we are not referencing them as `<namespace>.<entity>`. These endpoints are not namespaced, just split off into a separate file.

body:
unfortunately, this doesn't work because this assumes that the postgres container is ready as soon as the container starts. 
because of the db initialization process, postgres container is actually only ready after the `init-data.sh` script has executed, and the process has restarted after that.

I've added a correct health-check based condition [here](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/3369/commits/a4b160d85769a817175ff12619396da006711623).

body:
Nice work, @RicardoE105! I cannot wait to see the public API live. I've only commented at some high-level things so far, as you're still actively working on it. We should do another, more thorough review once you're approaching the finish line.

One more thing: Have you considered serving a [Swagger UI](https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-ui-express) right from the API, too? Also, I think a `v1/spec` endpoint serving the raw `openapi.yml` file would be handy.
author: csuermann

body: why is querystring encoding needed here? ­¤żö
author: csuermann

body: express >= 4.16.0 ships with a built-in [middleware](https://expressjs.com/en/4x/api.html#express.json) for parsing JSON bodies. Let's use that instead. But I believe express-openapi-validator already has built-in support for parsing bodies, so we may not even need it at all.
author: csuermann

body: `publicApiController` should encapsulate version handling for the whole public API and not refer to one particular version, e.g. `v1`.
author: csuermann

body: `x-eov-operation-handler` should point to the **versioned** handler function, e.g. `v1/users`. That way unchanged operations in the openapi spec for v2 could still simply refer to the old v1 handler.
author: csuermann

body: This is correct because it is in a try-catch, so we have to await the promise to make sure it did not error. In fact our ruleset currently [requires us to await](https://github.com/typescript-eslint/typescript-eslint/blob/v4.33.0/packages/eslint-plugin/docs/rules/return-await.md#in-try-catch).

body:
This is shared state. `initTestServer()` defaults this to `false`, but state persists among tests, forcing us to surgically reset this one property here despite being the default. 

Combining `jest.mock('../../config')` with `jest.resetAllMocks()` or with `jest.clearAllMocks()` or with `jest.isolateModules()` does not reset the mock state. 

Perhaps we could extract out the convict schema object (as done in the typings inferral PR) and then [`config.load(schemaObj)`](https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/blob/5d9148a2063e37382ef7e1672a47d1094afd70a1/types/convict/index.d.ts#L160-L163) at `beforeEach()`. Clearly out of scope, but please bear in mind since this happens in other tests as well.
author: ivov

body:
1. Curious why a chokidar setup instead of [esbuild watch mode](https://esbuild.github.io/api/#watch)? I expect this rebuilds on changes to any file, not just TS files, i.e. more than what we need?
2. Should we rebuild on `unlink`?
3. If `ignoreInitial` is `false`, it glitches out by re-detecting constantly. If `ignoreInitial` is `true`, then building before watching becomes required, i.e. if we watch without building first, then no `/dist` is created until the first change. This differs from the behavior of `tsc --watch`, which knows to build if there is no `/dist`.
4. Nitpick: Most watch tools give out some feedback e.g. `Listening for file changes...`, but this watch script is silent.

body:
Sorry for the delay! Re-review:

1. n8n fails to initialize - unable to load some esbuilt nodes:

```
ŌĆ©git reset --hard; git clean -dffx
lerna bootstrap --hoist; npm run build
npm run startŌĆ©
```


```
ŌĆ©> n8n@0.168.2 start
> run-script-os

> n8n@0.168.2 start:default
> cd packages/cli/bin && ./n8n

Initializing n8n process
 ŌĆ║   Error: There was an error: Cannot find module './v1/actions/router'
 ŌĆ║   Require stack:
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/nodes-base/dist/nodes/BambooHr/BambooHr.node.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/cli/dist/src/LoadNodesAndCredentials.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/cli/dist/src/index.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/cli/dist/commands/execute.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/node_modules/@oclif/config/lib/plugin.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/node_modules/@oclif/config/lib/config.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/node_modules/@oclif/config/lib/index.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/node_modules/@oclif/command/lib/command.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/node_modules/@oclif/command/lib/index.js
 ŌĆ║   - /Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/cli/bin/n8n
```

Removing Bamboo HR triggers same error for Cisco Webex and then Elasticsearch.

2. `.d.ts` are still being included, becoming `.d.js` in /dist. The glob in `build.mjs` is not excluding them: `console.log(tsFiles.filter(f => f.endsWith('.d.ts')));`
3. The existing TS build process emits CJS with the use strict directive; esbuild is [not doing this](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/422), but supports a [workaround](https://esbuild.github.io/api/#banner). 
4. Switching to a non-English locale causes the build to fail:

```
export N8N_DEFAULT_LOCALE=de
cd packages/nodes-base; npm run build
```

```
node:events:368
      throw er; // Unhandled 'error' event
      ^

Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open '/Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/nodes-base/dist/nodes/headers.js'
Emitted 'error' event on Domain instance at:
    at emit (node:internal/process/promises:134:35)
    at processPromiseRejections (node:internal/process/promises:242:25)
    at processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:97:32) {
  errno: -2,
  code: 'ENOENT',
  syscall: 'open',
  path: '/Users/ivov/Development/n8n/packages/nodes-base/dist/nodes/headers.js'
}
ERROR: "build:assets" exited with 1.
```

This issue is absent from `master`. The build stages are now parallel, so I expect the attempt to write to the headers path finishes first, failing to find the dist dir.

Edit: 4.5. Should we restore type-checking to the build process with [`--noEmit`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#noEmit)?

## Minor details

5. `'\n[esbuild] Watching for changes...'` is shown both for watch and for build, instead of just for watch. You can use the `watch.onRebuild` arg, refer to the second watch mode example:

```js
require('esbuild').build({
  entryPoints: ['app.js'],
  outfile: 'out.js',
  bundle: true,
  watch: {
    onRebuild(error, result) {
      if (error) console.error('watch build failed:', error)
      else console.log('watch build succeeded:', result)
    },
  },
}).then(result => {
  console.log('watching...')
})
```

6. Instead of removing the existing watch script reference in `package.json`, we should keep it as `watch:old` just like the old build script. In a few weeks if there are no issues, we can remove both.
7. After deletion of `watch.mjs`, `shelljs` is now only used for `rm -rf`. We already have `rimraf`, so `shelljs` might not be needed.
8. The only JSON files in /nodes-base/nodes and /nodes-base/credentials are codex files `.node.json` and potentially translation files. Both kinds are required cross-package from /cli, i.e. they are not directly imported inside /nodes-base, so I expect that `nodes/**/*.json` and `credentials/translations/**/*.json` should be removable from `tsconfig.json` without import issues.

```
find ./packages/nodes-base -type f -name \*.json ! -name \*.node.json
```

9. (Nitpick) Naming of `tsconfig.build.json` - the child only takes care of building /src, but both parent and child are responsible for the entire build, which makes the `.build.` infix for /src potentially confusing. This will become moot once we move src into /cli and skip declaration generation altogether.
10. (Out of scope, tech debt.) We should eventually explore the esbuild minify and tree-shaking flags.
author: ivov

body:
Thank you for the changes. Some final comments:

1. It is unusual that we only need to build `/nodes-base` in isolation. `/nodes-base` is almost always built together with all other packages  via `dev` in the root `package.json`, i.e. `lerna exec npm run dev --parallel`, which will run `npm run build` in `/nodes-base`. This means that `npm run build:fast` is unlikely to be used in day-to-day work. Should we add a root command for fast builds?

2. `skipLibCheck` will also skip checking of declaration files in the project itself, which is relevant because many nodes contain custom `.d.ts` files. The best of both worlds would be enabling this flag for the speed boost, but also converting all `.d.ts` files to `.ts` to ensure they're type-checked. Context: [1](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Performance#skipping-dts-checking) [2](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#skipLibCheck) [3](https://www.testim.io/blog/typescript-skiplibcheck/) [4](https://twitter.com/orta/status/1260583041740812288) Maybe we should consider delaying this flag until we can take the time to convert them.

3. `build.mjs` is throwing off ESLint:

```
Parsing error: "parserOptions.project" has been set for @typescript-eslint/parser.
The file does not match your project config: packages/nodes-base/scripts/build.mjs.
The extension for the file (.mjs) is non-standard. You should add "parserOptions.extraFileExtensions" to your config.
```

From a quick check, mjs needs to be [added to `extraFileExtensions`](https://github.com/typescript-eslint/typescript-eslint/issues/3383) or converted to `mts` which is supported by default, or else we could disregard mjs in `.eslintignore`.

4. `build:typecheck` is now inlining multiple flags to avoid using `--project`. These flags are a mix of settings already present in `tsconfig.json` e.g. `module`  and of settings absent from `tsconfig.json`, e.g. `downlevelIteration` and `allowSyntheticDefaultImports`. I am reluctant to introduce duplication and add new compiler flags that were not needed before and that required changes to the source as in Magento and Action Network, for a 2s gain. Commenting mostly to bring up the concern, but up to you.

5. Why switch `module` and `target` to ESNext? From the TS docs: `The special ESNext value refers to the highest version your version of TypeScript supports. This setting should be used with caution, since it doesnŌĆÖt mean the same thing between different TypeScript versions and can make upgrades less predictable.`

6. `fast-glob` is listed twice in `nodes-base/package.json`, as regular dependency and as dev dependency.

7. `"test/**/*"` is being included and `"**/*.spec.ts"` is being excluded but all tests in `/nodes-base` are JS.

```
git reset --hard; git clean -dffx
lerna bootstrap --hoist
time npm run build

system_profiler SPHardwareDataType
```

I get 2m 10s (first build) and 33s (cached build) on:

```
Model Name: MacBook Pro
Model Identifier: MacBookPro17,1
Chip: Apple M1
Total Number of Cores: 8 (4 performance and 4 efficiency)
Memory: 16 GB
```

Comparing against my [baseline benchmarks](https://linear.app/n8n/issue/N8N-3516#comment-104eaa6d) without esbuild and without turbo, only the cached build shows gains, but the first build is identical - this is surprising, as switching to esbuild in `nodes-base` should give us some gain.

2. We are removing `"nodes/**/*.json"` from `packages/nodes-base/tsconfig.json`, so with this new setup we no longer have codex files in dist. Therefore we should confirm that no external services rely on codex files in dist. For example, [`Supported Actions`](https://n8n.io/integrations/494-action-network/) and [`Basic Operations`](https://docs.n8n.io/integrations/nodes/n8n-nodes-base.actionnetwork/) are likely reading from codex files. Bringing this up mostly as I do not know offhand whether they would be affected - if they are reading from "untranspiled" codex files, then no issue.

body:
**General question**: If `^build` makes any package's `build` depend on its dependencies' `build` having completed first, [as explained here](https://turborepo.org/docs/core-concepts/pipelines#topological-dependency), then why do we need to specify our chain of dependencies by hand with the keys below? And why does the specified list of dependencies in `turbo.json` not reflect the dependency lists in `package.json` in our various packages?
- `design-system` does not depend on any package, but `turbo.json` lists `core` as a dependency.
- `editor-ui` depends on `design-system` and `workflow`, but `turbo.json` lists `core` as a dependency.
- `cli` depends on `workflow`, `core` and `editor-ui`, but `turbo.json` again lists `core`.

(Never used Turborepo before, there is probably a reason, mostly curious.)

**Smaller comments**:
1. Turborepo always needs `outputs` to be specified and is unable to find this in `tsconfig.json`?
2. `format`, `lint` and `lintfix` are not included in `turbo.json`. `test` is included, but it is still set to run with lerna in the root `package.json`.
3. We do not use `tsx`.
4. `full` is the [default output mode](https://turborepo.org/docs/reference/configuration#outputmode) and so does not need to be specified.
5. Re: `inputs`, not all code relevant to tests is in `/src` and `/test`, e.g.`/cli/commands`, `/cli/config`, `/nodes-base/nodes`, etc.
6. `"n8n-editor-ui#build"` When using `vue-cli-service build`, I notice it reports `Using 1 worker with 2048MB memory limit`. Since `editor-ui` is one of the slowest building packages, can we experiment with [adding workers](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62598443/how-to-increase-performance-when-building-vue-webapp) and [raising the memory limit](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55258355/vue-clis-type-checking-service-ignores-memory-limits)?

body:
Hello @Zebreus , thank you for your contribution, I did review and I'm not sure why batch operations needs to be dependent on continue on fail, I do not see any reason they should be a case but maybe I'm missing something. In any way we need to find a way to not do this. There are some other problems with upsert and delete operations, please take a look at comments above. Also some properties that depends on load methods need to updated with loadOptionsDependsOn.
If you have any questions please let me know.
author: michael-radency

body:
The schemas endpoint does only return custom objects, but the API documentation states that the objects API can also be used with contacts and other HubSpot objects. I did not find a Hubspot api endpoint, that gave me all objects, so I just hardcoded contact for now. You can generate the full list of possible objects with something like `seq 50 | xargs -n1 -i{} curl 'https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/schemas/0-{}?hapikey=INSERT_API_KEY_HERE' 2>/dev/null | jq '{name: .labels.singular, value: .objectTypeId }'`.

It would probably be best to add the full list here, as that would allow generic access to all HubSpot objects, but it would also clutter the list and make the custom objects harder to find.
author: zebreus

body:
When an element in a batch operation fails, the other elements still get processed. It seems to be common practice in most n8n nodes, to stop execution on error and not process any item after the erroring one.
The batch operations do process the upcoming items (because requests are batched), so the behaviour is not quite what the user would expect, when `continueOnFail` is disabled.
author: zebreus

body:
I want to return some additional details such as the id of the failed object, because just the error message returned by HubSpot is often generic. When `continueOnFail` is disabled, this is fine, because the whole response is displayed inside the spoiler. If `continueOnFail` is enabled only the message is shown and there is no way to retrieve further details about the error for logging.

For example any error during an update returns the message `Bad request - please check your parameters`. The response from Hubspot contains a lot more information about the error, to get and display that in a sane way it needs to be parsed.

The parsing can probably simplified a bit, but it should work for now.
author: zebreus

body:
The first check is just there, because the batch operations don't behave in the expected way, if continue on fail is not set. It could basically be removed and the code would still work.

This check checks it again because here it is actually wrong to parse additional information and return it if we don't `continueOnFail`. It cannot be removed.
author: zebreus

body: I added the complete list of all object types that are valid to use with the `crm/v3/objects` api. The `crm/v3/schemas` endpoint supports all object types, a different list should be used for that.
author: zebreus

body: When creating an object we cannot choose the HubSpot objectid, as it is always randomly generated. So we cannot create an object with a specific HubSpot objectid.
author: zebreus

body: No. If `idProperty` is set we are using a custom id property, which is not supported for all objects api operations. `DELETE` does not support it, but `GET` does, so a `get` request needs to be done first to resolve the custom id property id, to the HubSpot object id.
author: zebreus

body: done
author: zebreus

body:
Hey @michael-radency,
thanks for your review :rocket:. I have addressed all your comments above.

I left the threads for the continueOnFail check and the error parsing unresolved and stated the reasons for the decisions in the comments.

I have noticed that you added a operation that uses the schemas api. I think the schema operations should be summarized under a new ressource, because they do not operate with objects, but with schemas. Also the list of valid object types is different for the schemas and the objects api, as schemas can operate on nearly all types, while objects cant. I already have written code for resources for properties, property groups, and associations and will open a PR for that as soon as this PR is merged (Because the other commits depend on this). I think it would be best to include schemas there and not here.

The update operation lets the user specify they updated values as a fixedCollection of key/value pairs. One problem I noticed with that the node gets really sluggish when there are a lot of entries, I suspect it has something to do with the loadOptions method and the amount of options. Do you have any idea for an elegant solution to this?
author: zebreus

body:
I'm not sure if this should be used. Normally you'd `nack` to put it back in the queue so it's reattempted, but would there be a case where that would be helpful? If it errors out on the message parsing, it's not going to succeed on a second attempt right?

If we could nack the message if the workflow failed somewhere down the line, that'd probably be ideal, but I couldn't see a way to do that.
author: Ken-Michalak

body: I do actually not putting it back in queue but rejecting, second false. There is [this](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/2646) PR related to what you are suggesting, maybe you can take a look at
author: michael-radency

body:
Oh, nice. I didn't catch that. In that case, I don't see a reason that you wouldn't want to `nack` it, so you probably don't need the option. It should just always send that nack if it can't parse a message.

Does that deferred promise on the `emit` normally get the response of the last node in the workflow, and reject it if the workflow fails anywhere? I can take a look at implementing something like that for the emit later, but it's probably not needed to merge this in.
author: Ken-Michalak

body:
I think this is going to close every RabbitMQ connection when one workflow (actually one node) is stopped. The connections are 1 per set of credentials and running process, and should only be closed when the entire n8n process shuts down. (You could attempt to track the number of consumers per connection and shut it down, but I thought that would be difficult to track accurately and could lead to bugs.)

https://www.cloudamqp.com/blog/part1-rabbitmq-best-practice.html#connections-and-channels

When you have multiple workflows with rabbitmq nodes and multiple workers, you don't want to open and close a connection for every node, which is why there's a global map of connections.
author: Ken-Michalak

> I'm not sure if this should be used. Normally you'd `nack` to put it back in the queue so it's reattempted, but would there be a case where that would be helpful? If it errors out on the message parsing, it's not going to succeed on a second attempt right?
> 
> If we could nack the message if the workflow failed somewhere down the line, that'd probably be ideal, but I couldn't see a way to do that.

Just to be sure I understand: the aim would be to be able to wait until the end of the workflow, like for the webhook trigger

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/128426/189313388-78810340-5b72-471f-a815-6e62eb1e0d5f.png)

and based on the workflow's result (or some data returned by the last node in the workflow), decide to ack or nack the message, right?

if I understood it right, yes @Ken-Michalak that would be awesome if you could take a look and super useful for our use case

body:
`displayParameter` works as expected, i.e. for HTTPRN v1, it returns `false` for the cred of the same name that should be shown for v2. The issue is `find`. Because the creds in the two versions have the same names, and because the v2 creds are located first, `find` finds always the v2 cred for HTTPRN v1, and so `displayParameter` correctly reports that the v2 cred should not be shown for HTTPRN v1, without the cred with the same name in the other version being checked. Therefore, the fix filters creds by version before `displayParameter` is reached.

Can you explain why this is wrong?
author: ivov

author: alexgrozav

body:
To make this component even more reusable for the future, we should have a slot that we can use to place content at the top-right.

~~~
<slot name="actions" />
~~~

Then, we'd be able to use this callout component and put anything (not just a link) at the top right in the future.

~~~html
<n8n-callout>
  <template #actions>
                 <n8n-link
			:to="/something"
			size="small"
			theme="secondary"
			:bold="true"
			:underline="true"
		>
			Some text
		</n8n-link>
  </template>
</n8n-callout>
~~~
author: alexgrozav

body: We should use `v-bind="$attrs"` only on a root-level element, because this will assign all external attributes to the element and can lead to duplicate ids, repeating class names and unwanted attributes.
author: alexgrozav

body: Should keep this outside the component because it doesn't need to become reactive.
author: alexgrozav
alexgrozav

body:
Just like below, should have this inside a default slot for more flexibility. That way, we:
- can have multi-line text
- can have text emphasis (bold, italic)
- can have multiple actions
- don't need to emit custom events
author: alexgrozav

body:
UTF-16 strings can have characters like `%uXX` and `%uXXXX`. 

Essentially this has a lookahead for `%u` + a pair of optional hexadecimal characters `([0-9A-F]{2})?` + another `[0-9A-F]{2}` pair of hexadecimal values, or anything else `|.`.

Every row in `shared_credentials` stands for a sharing (i.e. a matching of a cred to a user) so I would name as `sharing` and adjust the function signature to reflect this. This will make the relation easier to understand to anyone unfamiliar, since a `SharedCredential` sounds by default like a `Credential` entity with a `shared` field.
```suggestion
		const sharing = await this.getSharing(user.id, credentialId, { includeCredential: true });
```

body:
Generally speaking, I think we should move away from `fail` in test descriptions for endpoints and either use the status code name or number, especially as endpoints can "fail" in more than one way. What do you think?

Being specific about the failure also makes the test report more descriptive (without making it longer) and helps to keep our expectations straight when for security reasons we intentionally return `403` instead of `404`, etc.

yes, unfortunately we are very inconsistent whether the whole user should be provided or just an id. In general I would prefer to always pass the whole object around, because you simply have more information and also typeorm is build for that style.

e.g.
`SharedCredentials.findOne({ user })` vs. `SharedCredentials.findOne({ user: { id: userId } })`

with `404` you would communicate that the request has failed because the resource was not found.

In this case we don't care about this, we just want to ensure that the resource is removed. Accordingly if the resource did not exist we achieved our goal that the resource is removed, thus a `200`

body:
1. For learning, why local storage instead of a Vuex store value coming from the `settings` column in the `user` table? Do we want to make it easy to keep this hint showing every time local storage is cleared?
2. Ideally, `dataItemsCount` integer should be checked before querying local storage.
3. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` Should we specify the medium `LOCAL_STORAGE` in the constant name? A generic name would prevent us from having to rename when refactoring later.
4. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` Does `INITIAL` add anything here? Discovery happens only once, at least until local storage cleared.
5. `LOCAL_STORAGE_PIN_DATA_INITIAL_DISCOVERY_WORKFLOW_FLAG` I find `CANVAS` instead of `WORKFLOW` more accurate + descriptive.
6. `hasSeenPinDataTooltip`, `showPinDataDiscoveryTooltip`, etc. Should we mention the medium `Tooltip`? A generic name like `Hint` prevents us from having to rename based on later refactorings, as had to be done for `.edit-mode-footer-buttons` ŌåÆ `.edit-mode-actions`
7. `checkPinDataDiscoveryFlow` The main purpose of this method is updating local state to show the hint, the checks are only there to make sure this happens in specific conditions. `showPinDataHint`?
8. Matter of preference, L365-7 could be a single line.
9. `5000` In my experience, 5 seconds was not nearly enough, because you first have to realize that there is a new tooltip to read, which is easy to miss in the NDV and especially when you're alt-tabbing back into the NDV from where you made the request to manually trigger the trigger node. Also the wording is not simple enough for a flashing tooltip: `You can pin this data to avoid having to generate an event every time.` Maybe 10 seconds with `You can pin the output of this node [Learn more]` with tooltip kept open on hover.

1. Agreed upon this with Mutasem and David. It was the easiest choice from an effort/result point of view.
2. Updated flow.

body:
From a DX point of view, I would expect our `n8n-checkbox` component to work with `v-model` as well, just like the `el-checkbox`.

Here we could bind the `value` prop of our component, and re-emit the `input` event. Also, don't forget add a `value` prop. 

~~~html
:value="value"
@input="$emit('input', $event)"
~~~

Having the `value` prop + an `input` event will allow us to use `v-model` on `n8n-checkbox`. Other than that, great job on this! :)

body:
Hey @vcrwr,

Just testing and I realised I messed up... This needs to be...

```
const credentialType = authenticationMethod === 'apiKey' ? 'hubspotApi' : 'hubspotAppToken';
return this.helpers.requestWithAuthentication.call(this, credentialType, options);
```

I forgot that the Hubspot node is one of the few where the authentication method doesn't match the actual credential name.

body:
1. Value extraction with `extractValue` relies on five other functions: `findPropertyFromParameterName`, `findPropertyInArray`, `extractValueRLC`, `executeRegexExtractValue`, and `extractValueOther`. Seeing as `NodeExecuteFunctions.ts` is already quite large, would it be reasonable to move all value extraction logic to a module that exports `extractValue`?
2. Is it really worth doing all this setup only for `options.find((i) => i.name === name);`? I would remove the helper and call `find` directly: `property = property.options?.find((i) => i.name === param);` Same for `values`.

For context, our ESLint rules came in after most of the existing code had been written, so most preexisting code was exempted and needs to be adjusted, while later additions should (ideally) not be disabling them.

That said, the lint rules are always up for discussion - we can even set special rules for decorators using the `custom` option for `naming-convention`, so it's up to us to decide how we want this. Personally I think `@GET` is the clearest and easiest to remember.

body:
Yes, it depends on what the login attribute is in the configuration. For example:

LDAP db record:

uid: ricardoe105
mail: ricardoespinoza105@gmail.com
sn: espinoza
givenName: givenName

If the login ID in the configuration is **mail**, then the email parameter will be the value **ricardoespinoza105@gmail.com**, and the email attribute value will be ricardoespinoza105@gmail.com

if the login ID in the configuration is **uid** then the email parameter will take the value **ricardoe105**, and the email attribute value will be ricardoespinoza105@gmail.com

I will change the name to login id for better readability. Also, I will revert the change to check whether it's the instance owner, as this only makes sense when the login ID is an email. It's ok to let the search fail and then check in handleEmailLogin again.

body:
> I just want to make sure that we're making the call 

Asking only in principle, to write better tests in future - in cases like this why do we want to ensure that these DB calls are made? My reasoning was that an implementation detail should not be tested, so that in future refactoring the implementation without changing the internal state will not require us to come back to adjust the tests.
author: ivov

body: In fact I am making this assertion because I want to assert that the business logic is being followed, such as creating a new object vs updating an existing. Also if we change the underlying implementation, as long as we continue using te same `Db.collections.Executions.<something>` the test should continue to work, but if we change it, the tests should also reflect that this business logic (saving vs updating) needs to be maintained.

body:
The issue here is that `npm ci` is installing dev-dependencies as well, which are needed for the `npm run build`. These are later deleted by the `npm prune`. 
If we split this up, we end up with a significantly larger image, as the layer with `npm ci` contains all the dev-dependencies, despite us not needing them after the build.

I plan to optimize these layers in the future to reduce the image sizes even further, but none of those plans fit into the scope of this PR IMO.

body:
Natively, the editor triggers six renders. The first three with an encoded angle bracket, which causes the expression to fail to resolve and so to be marked red as invalid. The next three use the unencoded angle bracket and so the expression resolves, but the `.invalid` class remains applied, hence red expression with green result.

Adjusting this line only as a workaround to ensure all renders always use the unencoded angle bracket, so that arrow function syntax is not wrongly marked invalid. Ideally we should look into why the editor needs to render so many times, but we are revamping the editor soon so I did not dig deeper. Let me know otherwise.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44588767/187848654-1386340a-c078-40ad-9115-793e4737f3f0.png)

body:
Hum that's a good point. Now thinking about alternatives, we have what we call "Activation errors".

You can see in the `packages/cli/src/ActiveWorkflowRunner.ts` file specifically inside `add` function, we try to activate a workflow and if it fails, we deactivate it and add an error the user can see when opening the workflows list.

Instead of generating a failed execution, what if we deactivate the workflow and display this error?

On the other hand, this is bad because we'll deactivate the workflow even if the service is temporarily down and a next poll request would work just fine.

Maybe the current approach you implemented is best for debuggability and failed executions are saved by default, so we should check this flag, yes, and only save if it's checked.

We just need to make sure that `WorkflowData` that we received was actually populated with this, because I've seen situations where this did not happen.
author: krynble

body: Yeah, I think this is a much better approach. For example, in the Airtable case, the issue is generated by changing the trigger column in Airtable (Workflow does not have to be touched). In case this happens, with this approach, the user will have an issue in the error tab or will be notified via the error workflow, then look at the error, and finally adjust in Airtable without having to touch the workflow. The other approach will be an extra step having to activate the workflow again, and if we do not save the execution plus they do not have an error workflow setup, they will never notice the error.

body:
@mutdmour I did some experimentation in a test repo with the [release process you pointed out](https://www.notion.so/n8n/Release-Process-fce65faea3d5403a85210f7e7a60d0f8#e91cea66bb774580b5fa0b073b2a3fc4) (thanks for that) and it does appear that conventional-changelog-cli is looking at non-merge-commits.

For example, even if you squash to one commit, and merge the PR, with a history on master like:
```
(merge commit with title from PR title) feat: fancy new feature
(normal commit from PR) my-non-conventional-commit-msg
...prior history...
```

This will **not** generate a changelog including `feat: fancy new feature`, in fact the changelog is empty.

But if you name normal commits conventionally as well, with a history on master like:
```
(merge commit with title from PR title) feat: fancy new feature
(normal commit from PR) feat: fancy new feature
...prior history...
```

The changelog is generated correctly.

So we'll need to lint non-merge-commit messages, not the PR title. This should be good for the times we want to review the git history as well.

I do want to stress that **you don't need to care about this until you're ready to merge**. If you used non-conventional commit messages, there'll be a failing check on the PR, but as soon as you squash to commit(s) with a conventional message, the check will start passing :+1: 

Let me know your thoughts, if you're happy I'll carry on implementing this to check all commit messages in a PR.

If we'd also want to soon lint commit messages in a pre-commit hook (e.g. with [commitlint](https://github.com/conventional-changelog/commitlint#what-is-commitlint)'s angular rules), it would be neat to extract both patterns from the same configuration to make maintenance easier.

Maybe if we'd factor in the optional scope for now as @mutdmour  proposed, and we can later look into making it fancier?
author: mieky

body:
> If you used non-conventional commit messages, there'll be a failing check on the PR, but as soon as you squash to commit(s) with a conventional message, the check will start passing

I believe in this case the checks would never pass before one would actually merge, as the "Squash & merge" step is when GitHub actually squashes the commits together, making the check pass.

In other words, the only way to make the checks pass *before* merging would be to manually squash and force-push them. Which I isn't a bad thing as such, but might make the barrier of entry a bit higher for casual contributors...

That said, I'm all in for favor of adding this check! Didn't test drive it myself yet, but seems fine!
author: mieky

- `xsmall` is `font-size-3xs`

This change means that we'll be keeping a copy of all JS and CSS files in the memory, and increasing the overall memory usage of every instance. However. this isn't a blocker. I can take care of optimizing these and setting up compression in another PR.

That did not work either - the problem is that the function returns a promise, but in order to return a promise it also awaits something else.

The returned promise only resolves after something else happens so we cannot await for it, but if we do await it throws an error after the first `await` resolves, which is also not catched. This is super odd

I tried having this in and it worked pretty nicely, but I'd actually advise against us leaving that in, as it adds moving parts and makes the thing a bit more difficult to maintain. I now finally left it out, but cleaned up the hint as redundant.

For reference, can be done like this:
```js
// Strip trailing slash and auto-append "/api/v1" if it doesn't exist
// (This needs to be in sync with the testRequest defined in N8nApi.credentials.ts)
baseURL: '={{ $credentials.baseUrl.replace(new RegExp("(/api/v1)?/?$"), "") + "/api/v1" }}',
```

I'm optimistic that the combination of the placeholder and the credential tester make it understandable enough for the user.

author: ivov

body:
The AST looks like:

```
CallExpression
  callee: MemberExpression
    object: Identifier
    property: Identifier
```

My approach is to narrow down what ESLint looks at and minimize upward lookups, e.g. in this case looking at every `CallExpression`, tolerating if deep copy, checking that `callee` is a `MemberExpression` having `JSON` and `parse`, getting ancestors and flagging.

When checking for `JSON` and `parse`, I'd also add look for `type` being `Identifier` - if you had written the rule in TS, it [would not have let you access `object` and `property` without this](https://github.com/ivov/eslint-plugin-n8n-nodes-base/blob/01b4ace0e93ef6aaafb90ad547eb22a5b02c4976/lib/rules/node-execute-block-missing-continue-on-fail.ts#L88-L89). It's very easy to write rules that crash the linter on an unexpected edge-casey AST, especially in raw JS.

I would really wish for this rule to be in TS and tested. If not this time, then let's open a followup story to do this later.
author: ivov

body: Agree. Maybe later we can alias this to `deepCopy` and try/catch any cyclic dependencies in there.
author: ivov

body:
I wholeheartedly agree with having the rules as TS and tests! I too discovered how having the rule written in Typescript already helps in catching some potential problems.

I was considering a tradeoff between having a single TS rule in a separate module (with a lot of boilerplate), and having the rule in JS stored locally, and this was just faster to get started with. It would be neat to combine the two, just not sure how the transpilation should be set up for it to be easy to use as a local rule (having it externalized would make this a non-issue). I'll think about this for a bit, let me know if you have good ideas dropping right off your sleeve ­¤ż×­¤Å╗ 

Also, great point about minimizing upwards lookups! I shall refactor this.

body:
They don't, but it's less verbose than passing them through via props.
1. `showNodeCreatorTabs` controls rendering of tabs inside MainPanel. If we'd store this state locally just inside `MainPanel`, we'd need to listen for an emitted event both from `TriggerHelperPanel` and `CategorizedItems`. And since `TriggerHelperPanel` is also using `CategorizedItems`, we'd also have to set up a listener there.
2. `showCreatorPanelScrim` in the upcoming PR, we'll show scrim only if the trigger tab is opened via the "Choose a trigger" node, so it'll be handy to only call if then.
3. `selectedNodeCreatorType` is again used in `CategorizedItems`, which are used both in `MainPanel` and `TriggerHelperPanel`, so we'd have to pass it through(MainPanel > CategorizedItems && MainPanel > TriggerHelperPanel > CategorizedItems)
I can create a standalone module for these node creator store props if you think it makes more sense

It didn't make much sense we update `draggablePosition` inside `onDrag` and then we set up a computed property with a dependency to `draggablePosition`

It feels like a performance penalty to watch a property which value is changed inside a mousemove handler so I wanted to a help a little on rendering by at least leaving this out from Vue event loop and setting it directly
author: cstuncsik

body:
`requestAnimationFrame` is the best way to keep animations smooth and also spare some CPU/GPU work.
By passing a callback you tell the browser: whenever it fits for your schedule please call this function (preventing it called more than it needs, imagine a how many times a function called during mouse move or scroll)
It's something similar than throttling but relying on browser repaint mechanism.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/window/requestAnimationFrame

@krynble If I understood right, we would have something `postReceive` definable right inside the pagination options instead of the current `rootProperty`? That's not a bad idea at all! It would enable one to pull off any kinds of operations that would be needed (even if I would suspect extracting the `rootProperty` might be the most usual case).

If anything, it would be nice if we could somehow reuse the same code for handling postReceives as elsewhere, as not to create too many execution paths. I could give it a try and see how it feels.

author: mieky

body:
Yup that's the idea @mieky 

But I'm also thinking that we can remove the `rootProperty` from here and use the `postReceive` array from the top level.

If you check the `INodePropertyRouting` type, you'll see it contains an `output` attribute, which is an instance of `INodeRequestOutput`. This already contains the `postReceive?: PostReceiveAction[];` property.

So as part of `packages/workflow/src/RoutingNode.ts`, the `makeRoutingRequest` currently receives as argument `requestOperations?: IN8nRequestOperations,` which does not contain all the information we want. If instead we pass a higher level object (which would be `INodePropertyRouting`) we would have all the information we needed.

If this is unclear LMK and we can chat about it!

body:
we really need to revisit our usage of `Object.assign`. it's seems to be used unnecessarily and incorrectly in many places. This should either be 
```
requestOptions.qs = Object.assign(requestOptions.qs ?? {}, { token: credentials.apiKey })
``` 
or 
```
requestOptions.qs = requestOptions.qs ?? {}
Object.assign(requestOptions.qs, { token: credentials.apiKey })
```
author: netroy

body: Moved to use a nullish check instead of `?? {}`, to remain closer to current behavior. `Object.assign(undefined, obj)` throws so we know the target is defined in these calls. Dug deeper and it looks like root cause is `Object.assign` overloads now [constraining the target param](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/commit/6e62273fa1e7469b89b589667c2c233789c62176#diff-c4b87a3c28600f5d77bbc99ce5062acfa048f028216e9d0a96adcf937887644e) to be a subset of `{}`.

on master often the slowest is `lint`, and the fastest is `test`.
should we change this to `test` -> `build` -> `lint` ?
author: netroy

body:
`test` requires `build` first, else we trigger [this issue](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/actions/runs/3158050095/jobs/5139642049). If we count `build+test` together, then `lint` is faster.

Or we could also do a partial `build` of only the packages needed by tests so that `test` can run first, and then `lint` and full `build` (or these two swapped around, mostly identical), but this option is prolly overkill for the gain.
author: ivov

body: `ts-jest` is supposed to transpile dependencies. so `test` requiring `build` sounds like a bug to me.

Unbuilt `n8n-core` cannot be found by `ts-jest` because it is symlinked from `node_modules/n8n-core` to `packages/core` because of workspaces. Do we want to avoid this symlinking to prevent this coupling?

Also I wonder if we could explore having `ts-jest` use the output of `build` to avoid having transpilation happen twice. Maybe as a separate PR.

body: I wonder if it'd be cleaner to use `if (error instanceof Error) {...}` or as a guard `if (!(error instanceof Error)) return;`
author: ivov

body: I was trying to avoid modifying logic in places I couldn't quickly test due to the amount of changes required.
author: valya

body: My reasoning is that we are already assuming we can access error-specific properties all over, so if the value being force-asserted `as Error` is not actually an error, the logic is bound to break or resolve to `undefined`, so `if (error instanceof Error) {...}` would only change logic to the extent that it would prevent those outcomes. But please correct me if I'm wrong.
author: ivov

body: Also another approach entirely would be to refactor the three spots where we are exempting [`no-throw-literal`](https://eslint.org/docs/latest/rules/no-throw-literal) to ensure we are always catching actual errors and not anything else, allowing us to keep the TS flag disabled.
author: ivov

body: The problem is a lot of places use parts of the errors then throw another more specific error up. Getting rid of `no-throw-literal` could work but it does mean no one is really thinking about what errors come back. Even though there's a lot of assertions at the moment it will mean whoever adds a new try/catch will have to think about what error is coming back for a second (well, hopefully they will).
author: valya

body:
> a lot of places use parts of the errors then throw another more specific error up

Can you link to an example of this to understand better? 

In my mind the goal of enabling this flag is to increase type safety by requiring us to refine the error type before usage in order to prevent us from relying on non-existing properties, and this is what type guards help with e.g. `if (error instanceof SomeCustomError)`, but maybe I'm not fully seeing the issue you mean.
author: ivov

body:
Independent of the rest of the conversation here, I'd like to point out that 
1. `(e as Error).message` has no advantage over `e.message` (when `e` is `any`). `instanceof` operator on the other hand adds a real check.
2. `no-throw-literal` helps us avoid strings being thrown in the code, but it does not help with any thrown strings coming from one of the hundreds of packages that we use.
author: netroy

body:
Thanks a lot for the PR @ivov 

Small changes suggested. One thing that I'm wondering about is whether or not we should think about an overlap of ownership to allow subworkflow execution.

Maybe it should behave just like credentials? Where at least one of the people with access to the workflow need to also have access to the used credentials. Perhaps implementing the same behavior here, where at least one of the people with access to the parent workflow must have access to the child. Probably not a requirement, but it's worth checking.

This endpoint is currently being used by the Executions list only, so we can be very conservative in the fields we allow here to simplify our logic and exclude possible loopholes.

body:
In this case, that's what makes sense (rendering `vue-json-pretty`) so I can see what's the real output.
Apart from this, I don't like `shallowMount` because it's cheating and sometimes can trick you.
In **Vue Testing Library** there is no such thing anyways, you have the `render` method which is basically the `mount` from **Vue Test Utils**, if you want to leave out a child component from rendering you have to explicitly mock it (`shallowMount` would do automatically for all children which also strange)

What I could do to make it nicer is that moved this import to the test setup file so no one has to worry about it again when testing a component that uses `vue-json-pretty`

4. We should aim for consistency in wildcard imports vs named imports, e.g. `import * as ActiveExecutions from '~/ActiveExecutions'` vs `import { ActiveExecutions } from '~/ActiveExecutions'`. I'd personally prefer named imports.

5. We should aim for consistency in index imports. `import { X } from '~/index'` vs `import { X } from '..'`. I'd personally prefer the more explicit variant.

6. We should aim for consistency in relative paths. `import config from './config'` vs `import config from '~/config'`. I'd personally prefer package-relative paths.

7. Should we lint to enforce that all relative paths are package-relative paths instead of regular relative paths? Can come later.

8. Some more path aliases might be useful, e.g. `~/databases/entities` ŌåÆ `@models`. Can come later.

body:
Seeing multiple type issues from cross-package imports. From a clean install and after restarting TS language server.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44588767/200281118-27dc9de7-da2a-4bb0-85f0-64e5960943b4.png)

> I also changed `~/` to `@/`, to be consistent with what the frontend code is using.

Matter of preference but I find it better to keep `@` for path shortcuts and `~` for package root, because `~` is clear in meaning and because using `@` for both package root and for shortcuts can lead to confusion: `@/someDir` vs `@someDir`. We can also change FE after discussion.

body:
By setting the initial value to `staticData[i]` here we effectively "save" in the state that we have executed it now, preventing it from being executed.

So to clarify, let's pretend that I activate a workflow now, 10th of November, 2022 and set it to run every Friday (tomorrow would be the first execution, from the day of writing). It should run every 2 weeks.

What's going to happen is that it won't run tomorrow, since we have said it has run already, and will only run 2 weeks from now, on Nov. 25, which is not what we want.

I think we should actually unset the `staticData[i]` so it's set after the first time it actually runs.
author: krynble

body:
If we do change the logic below to clear the initial value of `staticData[i]` then it will have the initial value of `undefined`.

Then the check below for it's definition would fail and we'd never run the correct calculations therefore set the initial value.

This also needs to be fixed.

body:
Yes, that could work also. I just like to think always about the performance but in this case, it's hard to tell if it is better to iterate through smaller regexes or just have one that covers all

Regex is always hard to read but as you become more experienced in it your eyes will immediately split it into chunks you can recognize, parentheses for the matching groups, brackets for characters etc...

For me, the merged regex is easier to read because as I start reading I'm already in scope for the parts whereas if I have to read to separate regexes (even if they're very similar) I can start interpreting it from the beginning again

body:
Found the following. No deep context so if any are intended, please disregard.

1. Stopping a running execution triggers two toasts: error for `Problem in node ŌĆśOn clicking 'execute'ŌĆś Workflow-Execution has been canceled!` (non-auto-dismissing) and success for `Execution stopped. Execution ID X` (auto-dismissing). I would think only the success toast is enough.
2. Selecting a stopped execution from the executions view triggers `Workflow-Execution has been canceled!`. I would not expect to see this toast again when selecting an old execution.
3. Retrying a stopped execution triggers `Retry unsuccessful` every time. The workflow is a simple Manual Trigger and Wait so it should be able to retry.
4. Selecting a failed retry of a stopped execution triggers `"Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'node')"`
5. Setting up a lengthy execution and switching to Executions view auto-selects the last already completed execution, but I would expect the running execution to be auto-selected.
6. ~~Disabling nodes in Workflow view is broken, have not tested if coming from master~~. Edit: Disregard, this is on master.

body:
I did exactly that first, but then I couldn't sort the tables in the order foreign key dependencies, and since we don't have `ON DELETE CASCADE`, truncating `role` threw an errors as `user` depended on it.

I already tried setting `PRAGMA foreign_keys=OFF`, tried doing the whole thing in a transaction as well, but none of these worked for some reason. 
If you know another way to disable foreign-key constraints on sqlite, I'd be glad to change this. I don't want any of us having to maintain this list either.

plus, if we can temporarily disable foreign-key constraints, we can fire these queries in parallel, and make this a few miliseconds faster ­¤śģ

body:
`connections` is `null` in he objects created in `/rest/workflows` because we don't query for that column. This is a hacky way to disable hash calculation for all the workflows objects for the listing call. 
Anywhere else where we fetch all the columns, `connections` never falsey, and the hash calculation works as usual.

body:
@netroy, what about setting mfaSecret and mfaRecoveryCodes as `select: false`, and querying them in the MFA endpoints only, which is the only place where mfaSecret and mfaRecoveryCodes are needed?

> select: boolean - Defines whether or not to hide this column by default when making queries. When set to false, the column data will not show with a standard query. By default column is select: true

Considering the size of the users table, I think we can handle that split via a migration.

1. The first condition would stop users for ever login in in they have MFA enable, because with MFA you do not login with both, token and recovery code, but with only one of then. So this will always throw error.

body: Now thinking about it, this may no longer be necessary. It has happened in some weird scenarios when going back and forward in history that the same connection command would end up in undo due to jsplump connections listeners so this prevents it. This may have stopped now that redo is cleared on each new user action which simplifies navigation options. Will have to check, but the generic approach is the way to go here for sure.
author: MiloradFilipovic

body: I don't like it ether, but found out not all connections are added and removed ind `__addConnection` and `removeConnection` methods, some are added and removed in those events. For example, if you drag a connection on an empty canvas space, it is removed in the `connectionDetached` event (among other situations, hence the flag), also when reconnecting nodes, some connections are pushed straight to store in `conncection` event. This made implementation rely on those flags which is a bit of chasing my own tail situation but I couldn't figure out better way to cover all cases.
author: MiloradFilipovic

body: Nope, bulk action inside this function is used when deleting a single connected node. In that case, it will contain `removeNode` and `removeConnection` commands. When deleting multiple nodes, this function is called in a loop and recording is started outside of this loop, so starting and stopping recording in each iteration will break the history recording.

body: The only purpose of tracking pane width was to prevent blur on resize, but seeing as this requires adding to state, watching, comparing to prior width, and possible issues with NDV-to-NDV jumps, I [refactored this](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/4814/commits/dda84915e80843bdc90c0b6b2ef5dd8c6d48aa26) to rely only on the presence of the `resizer` class. This no longer tracks anything and does not fire events on resize, which also (apparently?) even makes dragging to resize snappier than before because we are no longer triggering state changes. Let me know what you think.

body:
[We recently added support for custom root certificates](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/8705).
Instead of adding support for `Ignore SSL Issues` on every self-hostable node and credential, we'd prefer if all self-hosters could create a custom root CA certificate, generate all their self-signed certificates from that, and put the root cert in a docker volume mounted at `/opt/custom-certificates`. That way compromises on security a lot less, since certificate validation would still be enabled.

Could you try that option instead please?
author: netroy
